title: Warsaw Uprising
subtitle: The Polish Home Army's Battle for Liberation

axis.strength: SS-RONA Brigade, Dirlewanger Brigade, Wehrmacht and SS units

summary: The Warsaw Uprising began on 1 August 1944 as Soviet forces approached the Vistula. The Polish Home Army (Armia Krajowa) rose against the German occupiers hoping Allied support and Soviet advance would deliver liberation. The Soviets halted across the Vistula and provided minimal assistance, allowing Germany to suppress the uprising over 63 days. Hitler ordered Warsaw razed; systematic demolition destroyed 85% of the city.
context: The Polish Home Army (Armia Krajowa — AK) launched the uprising at 17:00 on 1 August 1944, when Soviet forces were only 10 km from Warsaw's eastern suburbs. The AK's plan anticipated Soviet cooperation, rapid air supply, and a brief battle before liberation. None came. Soviet forces halted on the Vistula's eastern bank for two months — reasons ranging from supply exhaustion to Stalin's calculated decision to allow the non-Communist AK to be destroyed before Soviet forces entered the city. The Dirlewanger Brigade (convicted criminals) and RONA Brigade (Soviet defectors) committed mass atrocities in the Wola and Ochota districts in the first five days, massacring an estimated 50,000–65,000 civilians. AK fighters held out in a collapsing city, fighting through sewers and across rooftops, but without heavy weapons, artillery, or reliable resupply. The AK capitulated on 2 October 1944. Hitler's retribution was systematic: the surviving population was expelled and German engineer units spent months demolishing Warsaw block by block — 85% of the city was destroyed before Soviet forces finally entered in January 1945.

casualties.allied: ~16,000 AK fighters killed; 150,000–200,000 civilians killed (including massacres); ~700,000 survivors expelled

outcome: German victory; Warsaw razed on Hitler's direct orders. 85% of the city deliberately destroyed.
significance: One of the greatest urban battles and tragedies of the war. The Soviet halt across the Vistula — whether strategic or political — remains deeply controversial and is central to Polish historical memory.

sources.ref: Instytut Pamięci Narodowej (IPN), Warsaw. File IPN BU 1572: Documentation of the Warsaw Uprising and post-uprising destruction.
Bór-Komorowski, Tadeusz. The Secret Army. Gryf, 1950.
sources.note: Memoir by the AK commander; the foundational first-person account.

sources.ref: Borodziej, Włodzimierz. The Warsaw Uprising of 1944. University of Wisconsin Press, 2006.
sources.note: The definitive scholarly account drawing on Polish, German, and Soviet archives.

sources.ref: Davies, Norman. Rising '44: The Battle for Warsaw. Macmillan, 2003.
